26: kai tote opsontai ton huion tou anthropou erchomenon en nephelais — and then they will see the Son of Man coming in clouds — the Danielic vision (Daniel 7:13) is cited in full; the coming-in-clouds is not spatial movement downward but the divine-presence-vehicle that indicates the Son of Man's authority-vindication. In Daniel's vision the Son of Man comes to the Ancient of Days (upward, receiving authority); in Mark's use the coming is toward the earth (downward, exercising the authority received). Both directions of travel are aspects of the same enthronement event.
1: lithoi / oikodomais — stones / buildings — The disciple's exclamation (ide potapoi lithoi kai potapai oikodomiai) reflects genuine awe: Herod's temple was an engineering wonder. Josephus reports that some of the retaining-wall stones measured 40–60 feet long, 10 feet high, and 14 feet deep. The temple mount platform covered 36 acres. The disciple's exclamation sets up the shocking reversal of Jesus's prophecy.
2: ou mē aphethē lithos epi lithon — not one stone shall be left upon another — the double negative ou mē with aorist subjunctive (aphethē) expresses absolute negation — an emphatic guarantee. The phrase lithos epi lithon (stone upon stone) is the exact reversal of what the disciple just admired. Fulfilled in 70 CE when Titus's legions destroyed the temple. Josephus describes the Roman soldiers dismantling even the foundations to remove hidden treasure and leave no basis for rebuilding.
3: kat' idian / katēnanti tou hierou — privately / opposite the temple — The private setting (kat' idian) with the inner circle (Peter, James, John, Andrew) mirrors other private revelatory moments in Mark (Mark 4:10–20; Mark 7:17; Mark 9:28). The Mount of Olives opposite the temple (across the Kidron Valley) was the site from which Ezekiel's glory-cloud departed (Ezekiel 11:23) and to which Zechariah expected the LORD to return for judgment (Zechariah 14:4).
4: pote tauta estai / to sēmeion hotan mellē tauta sytelesthai panta — when will these things be / what will be the sign when all these things are about to be fulfilled — the disciples ask two questions: (1) timing (pote); (2) sign (sēmeion). The Olivet Discourse responds to both but in ways that complicate simple identification. Synteleisthai (to be completely fulfilled, brought to completion) is an eschatological term with LXX resonance (Daniel 12:7).
5: blepete mē tis hymas planēsē — see that no one leads you astray — blepete (watch out/see to it) opens the discourse and frames its dominant concern: not curiosity about end-time schedules but vigilance against deception. Planēsē (lead astray, deceive) from planaō — the same root as planāsthe (you are led astray) used against the Sadducees (Mark 12:24). False teaching is the primary danger.
6: egō eimi / pollous planēsousin — I am he / they will deceive many — the false figures who come claiming egō eimi (I am he — a Messianic self-claim) are the negative counterpart to Jesus's own truthful egō eimi before the High Priest (Mark 14:62). The deception is precisely the use of legitimate language for illegitimate claims. The warning is not merely about pretenders but about the dynamic of religious charisma divorced from truth.
7: dei genesthai / telos — must happen / the end — dei (it is necessary, divine necessity) — the same word Mark uses for Jesus's passion (Mark 8:31). The wars and rumors of wars are ordained events, not signs of imminent final consummation (oupō to telos, but the end is not yet). The disciples' tendency to read catastrophe as eschatological signal is deliberately corrected.
8: seismoi / limoi / archē ōdinōn — earthquakes / famines / beginning of birth pangs — ōdinōn (birth pangs, labor pains) is a rich OT image for eschatological tribulation (Isaiah 26:17; Jeremiah 22:23; Micah 4:9–10; 1 Thessalonians 5:3). The word suggests not the end but the onset of a process that will culminate in new life — a painful but purposive suffering. The wars, earthquakes, and famines of Mark 13:7–8 are archē (beginning, first stage), not the end itself.
9: paradōsousin / synedria / synagōgas / hēgemonōn / eis martyrion autois — they will hand over / councils / synagogues / governors / for a testimony to them — paradidōmi (hand over, betray) — the same verb used for Jesus's betrayal (Mark 9:31; Mark 10:33; Mark 14:10–11). The disciples' path mirrors Jesus's. Eis martyrion autois (for a testimony to them) — persecution becomes the occasion for witness; martyrion is the root of the English word martyr.
10: prōton dei kērychthēnai to euangelion eis panta ta ethnē — first the gospel must be proclaimed to all the nations — prōton (first) signals a sequence: before the end, universal proclamation must occur. Ethnē (nations, Gentiles) — the scope is global. This verse functions as both commission and assurance: world mission is not human initiative but divinely ordained necessity (dei). The Olivet Discourse thus embeds the Great Commission within its eschatological framework.
11: mē promerimnate / to pneuma to hagion — do not worry beforehand / the Holy Spirit — promerimnate (pre-worry, be anxious in advance) — only here in the NT. The prohibition targets anticipatory anxiety, not thoughtful preparation. The promised substitute for human eloquence is the Holy Spirit speaking through the disciples — an implicit pneumatology embedded in persecution-discourse.
12: paradōsei adelphos adelphon eis thanaton — brother will betray brother to death — family betrayal is one of the signs of ultimate social breakdown that Jewish apocalyptic literature associated with the end (Micah 7:6; 1 Enoch 99–100). The verb paradidōmi (betray/hand over) appears again, linking discipleship-persecution to Jesus's own passion.
13: misou menoi hypo pantōn / ho de hypomonōn eis telos sōthēsetai — being hated by all / the one who endures to the end will be saved — universal hatred (hypo pantōn, by all) is the extreme social consequence of bearing Jesus's name (dia to onoma mou). Hypomonō (to endure, remain under, persevere) is the word for active patient endurance under pressure, not passive resignation. Sōthēsetai (will be saved) — the final passive divine action that rewards faithful endurance.
14: bdelygma tēs erēmōseōs / ho anaginōskōn noeitō — the abomination of desolation / let the reader understand — the phrase quotes Daniel 9:27; Mark 11:31; Mark 12:11 (bdelygma erēmōseōs, the abomination that makes desolate). The original referent was Antiochus IV Epiphanes's desecration of the altar (167 BC; 1 Macc Mark 1:54). Jesus projects the pattern forward to a future desecration. Mark's parenthetical ho anaginōskōn noeitō (let the reader understand) is remarkable — it breaks the narrative's fourth wall to call for interpretive discernment. Hestēkota hopou ou dei (standing where it ought not) uses a neuter participle for something that may be personal, which has led commentators to see a deliberately veiled reference.
15: mē katabatō / mēde eiselthō arai ti — let him not come down / nor enter to take anything — the urgency is absolute: no time for household retrieval. The flat-roofed Palestinian house had an exterior staircase to the roof. The instruction to flee directly from the roof without descending into the house conveys eschatological speed — no earthly possession justifies even momentary delay.
16: eis ta opisō epistrephasō arai to himation autou — let him not turn back to take his cloak — himation (cloak, outer garment) was a working man's most valuable single possession, serving as blanket and raiment. Even this must be abandoned. The instruction echoes Lot's flight from Sodom (Genesis 19:17: mē periblepēs eis ta opisō, do not look back).
17: tais en gastri echousais / tais thēlazousais — those having in the womb / those nursing — both phrases refer to the most physically vulnerable: pregnant women whose mobility is limited and nursing mothers who cannot easily flee. The lament (ouai, woe) is not a curse but an expression of compassion for those for whom flight will be most dangerous.
18: proseuchesthe / hina mē genētai cheimōnos — pray / that it may not be in winter — cheimōn (winter) — Palestinian winter brought swollen, impassable wadis, cold, and scarce food. The command to pray for favorable flight conditions assumes that prayer can affect the circumstances of historical events — a notable eschatological-practical connection.
19: thlipsis / hoia ou gegonen toiautē ap' archēs ktiseōs — tribulation / such as has not been from the beginning of creation — the phrase echoes Daniel 12:1: thlipsis hoia ou gegonen ex hou gegonen ethnos heos tēs hēmeras ekeinēs. The superlative framing marks this as a unique, unrepeatable historical crisis. Ap' archēs ktiseōs (from the beginning of creation) extends the comparison to the whole of cosmic history.
20: ouk an esōthē pasa sarx / dia tous eklektous hous exelexato — no flesh would be saved / for the sake of the elect whom he chose — pasa sarx (all flesh) — the totality of human life. Ekolobōsen (he shortened, cut short) — divine intervention limits the tribulation's duration. The eklektoi (elect, chosen ones) — those chosen by God (hous exelexato, relative clause reinforcing divine agency) — are the reason history does not run to total destruction. Election here functions as a limit on catastrophe.
21: idou hōde ho Christos / ekeie — look, here is the Christ / there — the false Messiah-sightings (pointing: hōde = here, ekeie = there) are the deceptive signs that the discourse warned about in Mark 13:5–6. The instruction mē pisteuete (do not believe) is absolute — no sign-working Messiah appearing under conditions of tribulation is to be trusted.
22: pseudochristoi kai pseudoprophētai / sēmeia kai terata / apoplanān — false Christs and false prophets / signs and wonders / to lead astray — pseudochristos (false-Christ) is a word Mark apparently coins or inherits from early Christian apocalyptic. The coupling with pseudoprophētai (false prophets) mirrors the OT pairing of false prophesy with false kingship. Sēmeia kai terata (signs and wonders) — the credentials of prophetic authority used deceptively. Ei dynaton (if possible) — the conditional acknowledges divine protection of the elect even against miraculous deception.
23: hymeis de blepete / proeirēka hymin panta — but you — watch out / I have told you everything beforehand — the emphatic hymeis de (but you) sets the disciples apart from those who will be deceived. Proeirēka (I have foretold, said beforehand) — Jesus's prophylactic disclosure removes any excuse for being caught off guard. The whole discourse is simultaneously prediction and protection.
24: meta tēn thlipsin ekeinēn / ho hēlios skotisthēsetai — after that tribulation / the sun will be darkened — the temporal marker meta tēn thlipsin ekeinēn (after that tribulation) introduces a second phase. The celestial imagery — darkened sun, non-shining moon — cites Isaiah 13:10 (oracle against Babylon) and Mark 34:4 (oracle against Edom). In OT prophetic usage, such cosmic convulsions regularly describe the catastrophic fall of kingdoms and the overturning of the old order, not necessarily literal astronomical events.
25: hoi asteres esontai ek tou ouranou piptontes — the stars will be falling from heaven — Isaiah 34:4 (kai panta ta astra peseitai). The heavenly powers (hai dynameis hai en tois ouranois saleuthēsontai) being shaken echoes Haggai 2:6 and Hebrews 12:26. The cumulative OT cosmic-disruption imagery functions as the backdrop for the Son of Man's arrival — the old order must be shaken for the new to arrive.
27: apostelei tous angelous / episynaxei tous eklektous ek tōn tessarōn anemōn — he will send the angels / and gather the elect from the four winds — apostelei (will send) — the Son of Man dispatches agents of gathering, contrasting with false prophets who scatter. Episynaxei (will gather together) — the prefix epi- intensifies the gathering as comprehensive. Ek tōn tessarōn anemōn (from the four winds) — the four-compass gathering appears in Daniel 7:2 and Isaiah 11:12, evoking the eschatological regathering of dispersed Israel expanded to include all the elect.
28: sykē / hē klados autēs hapalos genētai — the fig tree / when its branch becomes tender — the fig tree parable here contrasts with the cursed fig tree of Mark 11:12–14, 20–21. There the fig tree's unfruitfulness symbolized the temple's failure; here the same species serves as a positive timing-signal. When the branch (klados) softens (hapalos, tender, supple) and leaves appear, summer is near — observable, reliable, knowable.
29: hotan idēte tauta ginomena / engys estin epi thurais — when you see these things happening / it is near at the door — the observable signs (tauta) indicate nearness. Epi thurais (at the doors) — a threshold image of imminent arrival. The disciples who asked for a sign (Mark 13:4) are given a conditional one: the signs within history point to what is near, while the ultimate day remains unknowable (Mark 13:32).
30: hē genea hautē ou mē parelthē / heos hou tauta panta genētai — this generation will certainly not pass away / until all these things have happened — one of the most debated verses in Mark. Genea normally means the contemporaneous generation (those alive when Jesus speaks). The double negative ou mē parelthē (will certainly not pass away) is emphatic. Most contemporary exegetes read this as referring to the events of 70 CE (the destruction of the temple and tribulation of Mark 13:5–23), with Mark 13:24–27 forming a distinct eschatological horizon. The verse as a whole stakes Jesus's prophetic credibility on near-term verifiability.
31: ho de ouranos kai hē gē pareleusontai / hoi de logoi mou ou mē parelthōsin — heaven and earth will pass away / but my words will certainly not pass away — the contrast of ouranos kai gē (heaven and earth — the whole created order) with logoi mou (my words) stakes a remarkable claim. The verbal formula ou mē parelthōsin (will absolutely not pass away) is the same verbal form applied to the generation in Mark 13:30. The permanence of Jesus's words exceeds the permanence of the cosmos.
32: peri de tēs hēmeras ekeinēs ē tēs hōras oudeis oiden / oude ho huios / ei mē ho patēr — but concerning that day or hour no one knows / not even the Son / but only the Father — this verse is among the most theologically significant in the Gospels. The graduated ignorance — no angel, not even ho huios (the Son) — limits Jesus's knowledge in his incarnate state. The early church found this verse difficult; some patristic writers handled it by distinguishing the Son's divine and human natures. The verse sits within the broader NT framework of kenosis (Philippians 2:7). The unknown hēmera kai hōra (day and hour) is precisely what makes watchfulness necessary.
33: blepete agrypneite / ouk oidate gar pote ho kairos estin — watch out, stay awake / for you do not know when the time is — agrypneite (stay awake, be vigilant — literally: be sleepless) intensifies blepete. The reason for watchfulness is explicitly the uncertainty of timing: ouk oidate pote (you do not know when). The discourse does not give the disciples a timetable — it gives them an posture.
34: hōs anthrōpos apodēmos / thyrōrō / grēgorē — like a man going on a journey / to the doorkeeper / to keep watch — the parable of the absent householder: he distributes responsibility (hekastō to ergon autou, to each his task), with special attention to the doorkeeper. Grēgorē (keep watch, stay awake) — this is the imperative Jesus gives to one specific servant but which the discourse applies to all.
35: opsē ē mesonuktiō ē alektorophōnia ē prōi — evening / midnight / cockcrow / early morning — the four Roman night watches. This specific enumeration is not incidental: in the passion narrative that follows, each watch will appear. Gethsemane is late night; Peter's denial occurs at cockcrow (alektorophōnia, Mark 14:68, 72); the early morning watch (prōi) brings Jesus before Pilate (Mark 15:1). The Olivet Discourse's watch-schedule is embedded in the passion's temporal structure.
36: exaiphnēs elthōn / katheudontas hymas heurē — suddenly coming / finds you sleeping — exaiphnēs (suddenly, unexpectedly — adverb of abrupt appearance) — the opposite of a scheduled, signaled arrival. Katheudontas (sleeping) — the disciples will fail to stay awake in Gethsemane (Mark 14:37–41) in a direct dramatic echo of this warning. The irony of their failure is prepared here.
37: ho de hymin legō pasin legō / grēgoreite — and what I say to you I say to all / Watch! — the discourse expands its audience beyond the four disciples: pasin (to all). Grēgoreite (Watch! — plural imperative) — the final word of the Olivet Discourse. This imperative will echo tragically in Gethsemane (grēgoreite, Mark 14:34, 38) where the disciples sleep three times while Jesus prays. The climax of the discourse is a one-word command that the disciples will fail within hours.
